claims.claim: MACC's May 2026 1MDB recovery headline was RM31.3 billion, equal to 74.5 percent of RM42 billion.
claims.source: Bernama report of MACC chief Azam Baki press conference, 6 May 2026: https://bernama.com/en/region/news.php/world/news.php?id=2553395
claims.correction: The figure is reported as RM31,301,717,268.56 recovered as of 2025, equivalent to 74.5 percent of RM42 billion. No direct MACC press release was found in search, so this is based on Bernama's report of the named MACC press conference.
claims.stale_check: Current as of the 6 May 2026 report found.

claims.claim: The RM31.3 billion MACC headline counts RM6 billion that Goldman Sachs is still fighting in London arbitration.
claims.source: Bernama/MOF written reply, 15 Oct 2025: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2478918; Bernama MACC report, 6 May 2026
claims.correction: It is verified that the Government and 1MDB listed Goldman Sachs' settlement at US$3.9 billion, or RM16.48 billion, and that LCIA arbitration over the Goldman settlement was still being handled in October 2025. However, the public sources found do not explicitly break down MACC's RM31.3 billion total to prove that the disputed US$1.4 billion guarantee is included as recovered in that specific May 2026 figure.
claims.stale_check: October 2025 MOF reporting shows arbitration remained ongoing.

claims.claim: Goldman Sachs went to the London Court of International Arbitration in October 2023.
claims.source: Bernama/MOF written reply, 15 Oct 2025: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2478918; Bernama Johari Ghani statement, 12 Oct 2023: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2234063
claims.correction: Use the precise date found in the official parliamentary reply as reported by Bernama: Goldman Sachs Group Inc filed an arbitration claim at the LCIA on 11 October 2023.
claims.stale_check: Later October 2025 MOF reporting confirms the arbitration was still being handled by Malaysia's legal team.

claims.claim: Goldman Sachs went to LCIA to argue it owes less.
claims.source: Bernama Johari Ghani statement, 12 Oct 2023
claims.correction: More precise wording: Goldman claimed Malaysia violated obligations to credit assets against the guarantee and to recover other assets; Malaysia said Goldman was trying to avoid an interim payment obligation of US$250 million.
claims.stale_check: The characterization remained relevant because October 2025 MOF reporting still described the arbitration as arising from obligations under the August 2020 settlement agreement.

claims.claim: If the arbitrators side with Goldman, the RM31.3 billion headline drops overnight.
claims.source: No primary source found stating that MACC would revise the RM31.3 billion headline if Goldman prevailed.
claims.correction: This should be framed as a risk, not a fact: If the disputed guarantee is included in the recovery figure and Goldman prevails, the recoverable or recognized amount may need clarification or adjustment.
claims.stale_check: No public arbitral award or official treatment of a hypothetical Goldman win was found.

claims.claim: Goldman's 2020 Malaysia settlement was US$3.9 billion.
claims.source: Goldman Sachs press release, 24 Jul 2020; MOF press citation, 26 Jul 2020
claims.correction: No correction required.
claims.stale_check: The settlement structure is historical and stable.

claims.claim: Of Goldman's US$3.9 billion settlement, US$2.5 billion was cash and US$1.4 billion was an asset-recovery guarantee.
claims.source: Goldman Sachs press release, 24 Jul 2020; MOF press citation, 26 Jul 2020
claims.correction: No correction required.
claims.stale_check: Historical settlement terms are stable, although the later execution of the guarantee is disputed.

claims.claim: The US$1.4 billion Goldman guarantee is about RM6 billion.
claims.source: MOF PDF press citation, 26 Jul 2020, using US$1 = RM4.26
claims.correction: At the RM4.26 rate used in the 2020 MOF press citation, US$1.4 billion is about RM5.96 billion. 'About RM6 billion' is acceptable if tied to the 2020 settlement exchange context.
claims.stale_check: Exchange-rate-sensitive.

claims.claim: As of February 2025, the most recent public update from Treasury's Datuk Johari Abdul Ghani was that the Goldman dispute remained under arbitration.
claims.source: Bernama/MOF written reply, 15 Oct 2025: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2478918
claims.correction: The February 2025 statement was not the most recent public update found. A later October 2025 MOF parliamentary reply stated the Government's legal team was handling the arbitration and detailed information could not be disclosed due to confidentiality obligations.
claims.stale_check: The analysis appears stale because it missed the October 2025 MOF update.

claims.claim: Malaysia paid RM43.8 billion servicing 1MDB debt by March 2023.
claims.source: MOF press citation of Bernama report, 13 Mar 2023
claims.correction: More precise wording: As at 13 March 2023, the government said it had paid RM43.8 billion of 1MDB debt.
claims.stale_check: Updated MOF figures exist for July and September 2025.

claims.claim: RM9.7 billion was still outstanding after March 2023.
claims.source: MOF press citation of Bernama report, 13 Mar 2023
claims.correction: Correct only as a March 2023 statement. It should not be presented as the latest figure.
claims.stale_check: Stale. MOF reported in August 2025 that outstanding 1MDB IMTN debt was RM9.02 billion; November 2025 reporting stated broader outstanding 1MDB obligations were RM24.46 billion including shareholder advances.

claims.claim: Neither the RM43.8 billion paid nor the RM9.7 billion outstanding had been separately updated since March 2023.
claims.source: Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 14 Aug 2025: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2456412; Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 27 Nov 2025: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2496226
claims.correction: MOF updated the position in August 2025: RM42.17 billion net fund disbursed up to 31 July 2025, with RM15.44 billion from MOF/MOF Inc funds and RM26.73 billion from recovered assets; outstanding 1MDB IMTN debt was RM9.02 billion. MOF also reported in November 2025 that outstanding 1MDB obligations were RM24.46 billion including shareholder advances.
claims.stale_check: This is a clear stale-critique failure.

claims.claim: MACC's May 2026 cumulative recovery figure is RM31.3 billion.
claims.source: Bernama report of MACC chief Azam Baki press conference, 6 May 2026
claims.correction: No correction required, subject to noting this was Bernama's reporting of a MACC press conference.
claims.stale_check: Current as of 6 May 2026.

claims.claim: Through March 2023, the public account was at least RM12.5 billion in the hole on 1MDB before counting the RM6 billion in arbitration.
claims.source: MOF press citation, 13 Mar 2023; Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 14 Aug 2025; Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 27 Nov 2025
claims.correction: The simple subtraction RM43.8 billion minus RM31.3 billion is not a reliable net-fiscal calculation because debt servicing was partly funded by recovered assets and later MOF figures show a different structure. The better supported framing is: as of September 2025, MOF reported RM31.2 billion recovered into the Asset Recovery Trust Account, RM5.29 billion remaining in that account, and RM24.46 billion in outstanding 1MDB obligations including shareholder advances.
claims.stale_check: Stale and misleading because it ignores August and November 2025 MOF updates.

claims.claim: The RM42 billion figure is 1MDB's accumulated debt at mid-2015, not what the US Justice Department says was stolen.
claims.source: PAC report on 1MDB, 2016; DOJ press release, 13 Jun 2024
claims.correction: RM42 billion has historically been associated with 1MDB debt. However, the May 2026 MACC statement itself reportedly called RM42 billion the amount misappropriated. The analysis should say: RM42 billion has historically been associated with 1MDB debt, while DOJ uses a different baseline of more than US$4.5 billion allegedly misappropriated.
claims.stale_check: The distinction remains important, but the analysis overstates the certainty of the RM42 billion denominator's origin in the May 2026 MACC framing.

claims.claim: The DOJ established that US$4.5 billion was misappropriated between 2009 and 2015.
claims.source: DOJ press release, 13 Jun 2024; DOJ press release, 17 Jan 2025
claims.correction: Use DOJ's own legal qualifier: DOJ says that, as alleged in civil forfeiture complaints, from 2009 through 2015 more than US$4.5 billion in 1MDB funds were misappropriated. 'Established' and 'proven' are stronger than the cited DOJ wording.
claims.stale_check: DOJ repeated the same allegation in January 2025; no later source found changing the baseline.

claims.claim: Against the DOJ baseline, the RM31.3 billion headline implies the proven misappropriation has been more than fully recovered.
claims.source: DOJ press release, 13 Jun 2024; Bernama MACC report, 6 May 2026
claims.correction: Do not say 'proven misappropriation' based on the DOJ press release. Also, RM31.3 billion appears to include asset recoveries and settlements, while DOJ's US$4.5 billion is an alleged misappropriation baseline. These are not directly comparable without a reconciliation table.
claims.stale_check: The comparison remains methodologically weak.

claims.claim: RM31.3 billion is real money returned to Malaysia.
claims.source: Bernama MACC report, 6 May 2026; Bernama/MOF written reply, 15 Oct 2025
claims.correction: Use 'assets recovered' or 'value recovered' unless a source confirms all of the RM31.3 billion is cash received. MACC reported total value of assets recovered, not necessarily cash in the till.
claims.stale_check: May 2026 reporting used 'assets recovered' and included settlements, assets, and cross-border recoveries.

claims.claim: The RM31.3 billion recovery is one of the largest financial-crime recoveries on record.
claims.source: Bernama report quoting MACC chief, 6 May 2026
claims.correction: This can be attributed to MACC's claim, but should not be stated independently unless benchmarked against a global asset-recovery dataset.
claims.stale_check: No global comparative primary dataset was verified.

claims.claim: The honest reading is that most of what was provably stolen has been clawed back.
claims.source: DOJ press release, 13 Jun 2024; Bernama MACC report, 6 May 2026
claims.correction: Replace 'provably stolen' with 'alleged by DOJ to have been misappropriated' or 'reported by MACC as misappropriated'. The word 'provably' overstates the DOJ source language and conflates criminal proof, civil forfeiture allegations, settlements, and recovery accounting.
claims.stale_check: No source found justifying 'provably' as applied to the whole US$4.5 billion or RM42 billion.

claims.claim: The net-fiscal position is still negative.
claims.source: Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 27 Nov 2025
claims.correction: The stronger support is the September 2025 MOF position: RM5.29 billion remained in the Asset Recovery Trust Account while outstanding 1MDB obligations were RM24.46 billion, including IMTN principal/interest and shareholder advances.
claims.stale_check: September 2025 MOF data is newer and more precise than the March 2023 debt-service figure.

claims.claim: MOF October 2025 reported RM20.73 billion from banks only.
claims.source: Bernama/MOF written reply, 15 Oct 2025: https://www.bernama.com/en/news.php?id=2478918
claims.correction: The reported figure is RM20.71 billion, not RM20.73 billion: Goldman Sachs US$3.9 billion or RM16.48 billion, AmBank RM2.83 billion, and JP Morgan Switzerland RM1.4 billion.
claims.stale_check: No later correction to the RM20.71 billion figure was found.

claims.claim: The yearly recovery breakdown from 2019 to 2025 sums to RM31.298 billion.
claims.source: Bernama MACC report, 6 May 2026
claims.correction: The rounded annual figures sum to about RM31.299 billion, while MACC's precise total is RM31,301,717,268.56.
claims.stale_check: Current as of 6 May 2026.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: MACC's RM31.3 billion figure counts the disputed Goldman US$1.4 billion guarantee as recovered.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The available public sources verify the Goldman settlement structure and the arbitration, but do not explicitly reconcile MACC's RM31.3 billion total to show the disputed guarantee is included.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: The DOJ established that US$4.5 billion was misappropriated.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: DOJ's release says 'as alleged in the civil forfeiture complaints'. The analysis converts an allegation baseline into an established or proven baseline.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Through March 2023, the public account was at least RM12.5 billion in the hole by subtracting RM31.3 billion recovery from RM43.8 billion debt paid.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: This compares non-like-for-like line items and ignores that debt servicing was partly funded by recovered assets.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 14 Aug 2025; Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 27 Nov 2025

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Neither the RM43.8 billion debt-service figure nor the RM9.7 billion outstanding figure has been separately updated since March 2023.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: MOF issued later parliamentary replies in August and November 2025 updating the debt, funding-source, recovery, and outstanding-obligation picture.
cardinal_sin_flags.primary_source: Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 14 Aug 2025; Bernama/MOF parliamentary reply, 27 Nov 2025

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: Against the DOJ baseline, the RM31.3 billion headline implies the proven misappropriation has been more than fully recovered.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The DOJ baseline is alleged, exchange-rate-sensitive, and not necessarily comparable to Malaysia's recovery and settlement accounting.

cardinal_sin_flags.claim: RM31.3 billion is real money returned to Malaysia.
cardinal_sin_flags.why: The MACC report refers to total value of assets recovered and includes settlements and assets. It does not prove that all RM31.3 billion is cash already received.

omitted_facts: MOF updated the 1MDB debt-service picture in August 2025: RM42.17 billion net fund had been channelled to 1MDB up to 31 July 2025, of which RM15.44 billion came from MOF/MOF Inc funds and RM26.73 billion from recovered 1MDB assets.
MOF reported in August 2025 that RM28.93 billion was used for principal debt repayment and RM13.24 billion for interest payments and company commitments.
MOF reported in August 2025 that outstanding 1MDB IMTN debt was RM9.02 billion, not the earlier March 2023 RM9.7 billion figure.

MOF reported in November 2025 that RM31.2 billion linked to 1MDB and SRC had been recovered into the Asset Recovery Trust Account, with RM5.29 billion remaining and RM24.46 billion in outstanding 1MDB obligations including shareholder advances.
The March 2023 Ahmad Maslan report itself said the RM43.8 billion debt payment was made partly through a trust account, so describing the full RM43.8 billion as taxpayer-funded is not supported.
The October 2025 MOF reply said all cash proceeds from the three financial institutions had been received in full, while also stating that Goldman arbitration was ongoing and confidential.
The DOJ source uses 'as alleged in civil forfeiture complaints' for the more-than-US$4.5 billion misappropriation figure.
The source list includes Wikipedia, which is not a primary source under the stated fact-checking rules and should not be used to validate the denominator history.
source_assessment: Strongest sources: Goldman Sachs' 2020 press release, MOF-hosted press citations, Bernama reports of MOF parliamentary replies, DOJ press releases, PAC document. Weakest part of the source base is the May 2026 MACC number, because verification relies on Bernama and Malay Mail reporting of the press conference. Overall source diversity acceptable across Malaysian official sources, US DOJ, and Goldman, but weak for independent benchmarking of 'largest recovery' and weak for the exact internal composition of MACC's RM31.3 billion figure.
overall_assessment: Legal settlement structure mostly accurate. Major weaknesses in framing and staleness. The economic card is materially flawed because it says the March 2023 debt figures were not updated, when MOF issued later August and November 2025 updates. The net-fiscal conclusion is directionally supportable only if reframed using MOF's later outstanding-liability data. The governance card correctly identifies a denominator problem, but overclaims by saying DOJ 'established' or 'proved' the US$4.5 billion amount. Revise before publication: remove unverified certainty around the disputed Goldman guarantee, replace 'real money' with 'reported recovery value', update the MOF debt-service section with 2025 data, and clearly distinguish debt, alleged misappropriation, recovered assets, settlements, cash received, guarantees, and outstanding liabilities.
